segments.text: From Middle English nexte , nexste , nixte , from Old English nīehsta , nīehste , etc. , inflected forms of nīehst
The man in the next bunk kept me awake all night with his snoring .
She lives a mile or two away , in the next village .
Most direct , or shortest or nearest in distance or time .
Nearest in order , succession , or rank ;
Please turn to the next page .
On Wednesday next , I 'm going to Spain .
the next chapter ; the next week ; the Sunday next before Easter
The man was driven by his love for money and his desire
to become the next Bill Gates .
Near was originally the comparative form of nigh ;
Nigh is used today mostly in archaic , poetic , or regional contexts .
See also Thesaurus : former or Thesaurus : subsequent
immediately following the current or most recent one .
Next week would be a good time
( of days of the week or months of the year )
Closest in the future , or closest but one
if the closest is very soon ;
of days , sometimes thought to specifically refer to the instance closest to seven days
do you mean Thursday this week or Thursday next week ?
In a time , place , rank or sequence closest or following .
They live in the next closest house .
It 's the next best thing to ice cream .
So as to follow in time or sequence something
First we removed all the handles ;
next , we stripped off the old paint .
Financial panic , earthquakes , oil spills , riots .
On the side of ; nearest or adjacent to ; next to .
do n't hold up the queue !
( “ group of persons , multitude , host , school of fish ” ) ,
( “ multitude , troop of people , swarm of animals ” ) ,
Cognate with Middle Low German schōle
( “ troop or band of people , host , multitude , school of fish ” ) .
A group of fish or a group of marine mammals such as porpoises , dolphins , or whales .
The divers encountered a huge school of mackerel .
( third - person singular simple present schools ,
simple past and past participle schooled )
To form into , or travel in a school .
From Middle English scole , from Old English scōl
( “ learned discussion or dissertation , lecture , school ” ) ,
( skholḗ , “ spare time , leisure ; conversations and the knowledge
gained through them during free time ;
( West Frisian skoalle , Saterland Frisian Skoule ) ,
Influenced in some senses by Middle English schole
Related also to Old High German sigi
Our children attend a public school in our neighborhood .
Harvard University is a famous American postsecondary school .
At Eton College , a period or session of teaching .
Divinity , history and geography are studied for two schools per week .
Within a larger educational institution , an organizational unit , such as a department or institute ,
which is dedicated to a specific subject area .
We are enrolled in the same university ,
but I attend the School of Economics
and my brother is in the School of Music .
An art movement , a community of artists .
The Barbizon school of painters were part of an art movement towards Realism in art ,
which arose in the context of the dominant Romantic Movement of the time .
The followers of a particular doctrine ; a particular way
or particular doctrine ; a school of thought .
These economists belong to the monetarist school .
during which classes are attended or in session in an educational institution .
The room or hall in English universities
where the examinations for degrees and honours are held .
The canons , precepts , or body of opinion or practice ,
sanctioned by the authority of a particular class or age .
He was a gentleman of the old school .
offering specialized instruction , as for driving , cooking , typing , coding , etc.
( organizational unity within an educational institution ) :
nursery school , kindergarten , college , polytechnic , university
( third - person singular simple present schools ,
simple past and past participle schooled )
( often , but not necessarily , in a school ) .
Many future prime ministers were schooled in Eton .
to teach an opponent a harsh lesson .
To control , or compose , one 's expression .
She took care to school her expression ,
not giving away any of her feelings .
Long considered a borrowing from Old Norse traust
but the root vocalism is incompatible ,
and now it 's considered a reflex of an unattested Old English *trust , from a rare zero - grade proto - Germanic variant of the same root
also attested in Middle High German getrüste
Confidence in or reliance on some person or quality .
He needs to regain her trust
if he is ever going to win her back .
Dependence upon something in the future ; hope .
Confidence in the future payment for goods or services
but the landlady let me have it on trust .
The condition or obligation of one
who has legal ownership of a property
to manage for the benefit of another .
I put the house into my sister 's trust .
whereby property or money is given
to be held by a third party
that it will be managed for the benefit of , or eventually transferred to , a stated beneficiary ;
to be given to a child
when he or she reaches adulthood .
A group of businessmen or traders
to produce and distribute specific commodities or services ,
and managed by a central body of trustees .
Affirmation of the access rights of a user of a computer system .
( confidence in a person or quality )
( third - person singular simple present trusts ,
simple past and past participle trusted )
to rely on for continuing support or aid .
( usually with a phrase or infinitive clause as the object )
to show confidence in a person
To commit , as to one 's care ;
to sell to upon credit , or in confidence of future payment .
Merchants and manufacturers trust their customers annually with goods .
he had to trust to his memory for further details .
To sell or deliver anything in reliance upon a promise of payment ;
of or relating to a trust .
